title: Shortcomings and capacities of real-constrained neural networks in complex spaces
abstract: We find the asymptotic ratio between the storage capacities when enforcing real pre-activations in a complex hypothesis class as opposed to complex ones in the same class. Our methods depend on Gardner volume comparisons at critical capacity. Our proof relies on an application of the Harish-Chandra-Itzykson-Zuber (HCIZ) formula, nonstandard in literature. With the HCIZ formula, we may obtain a more robust approximation for the final asymptotic ratio. This strategy is applicable to our work specifically since we integrate over the unitary and orthogonal compact manifolds, facilitated via the Weyl integration formula and the Haar measure.
summary: This theoretical paper calculates the asymptotic ratio of storage capacities for complex neural networks when real constraints are imposed on the pre-activations. It employs sophisticated techniques from statistical physics, including the replica method and the Harish-Chandra-Itzykson-Zuber formula. The work provides a rigorous, albeit highly technical, analysis of model expressivity in complex spaces.
main_problem: The paper investigates the asymptotic ratio of storage capacities for complex-valued neural networks when the pre-activations are constrained to be real versus unconstrained complex values.
main_result: The authors derive the asymptotic ratio $\Gamma$ using advanced mathematical tools, suggesting a non-trivial dependence on the network structure and constraints.
method: The analysis heavily employs the replica method, saddle-point evaluations, and advanced mathematical identities like the Harish-Chandra-Itzykson-Zuber (HCIZ) formula for integration over compact manifolds.
model_or_system: The model is a complex-valued neural network analyzed in the context of its storage capacity and expressivity, utilizing concepts from statistical physics like the Gardner volume.
key_observables: Storage capacities ($\alpha_r, \alpha_c$), the asymptotic ratio ($\Gamma$), and the quenched free energy ($f$).
important_parameters: Sparsity constraint parameter ($ho$), generalization margin ($\kappa$), and the network size ($N$).
main_assumptions: The derivation relies on the replica symmetric ansatz, the exchange of limit orders, and the applicability of advanced asymptotic approximations like Laplace's method.
figures_summary: Figure 1 plots the ratio $\Gamma$ across varying parameters $ho$ and $G_{r,c}, B$, illustrating the theoretically valid regime $[0, 1]$.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the core problem (ratio of capacities), then developing the mathematical machinery using the replica trick and complex integration over unitary/orthogonal groups, culminating in the final asymptotic expressions for the capacity.
why_interesting: While focused on neural networks, the mathematical machinery—replica methods, integration over compact groups, and free energy calculations—is highly relevant to understanding phase transitions and complexity in many-body quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: We plot Γ across varying ρ and G{r,c}, B. We suppress values of Γ /∈[0, 1] in the plot. In theory, G{r,c}, B are not free parameters, and Γ /∈[0, 1] is not an actual scenario. We take X to be plot value and B′ = X −G′ r −∂ρGc. We attempt to choose G′ r, ∂ρGc, B′ consistent with a real scenario. The void space is plausible mathematically but does not represent a realistic scenario in regard to physical constraints on the spin glass system. We remark this plot is primarily a proxy for illustrative purposes to convey a typical Γ in its valid regime.
Figure 2: We plot the same as Figure 1 but we take ∂ρGc = X −B′ −G′ r for B′, G′ r fixed.
Figure 3: We plot the same as Figure 1 but we take G′ r = X −B′ −∂ρGc for B′, ∂ρGc fixed.
